coredata.dc:title: A novel Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs composite with spherical-chain-like morphology for efficient oxygen evolution reaction in aqueous alkaline solutions
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Fan
Sun, Beibei
Dong, Guohua
Liu, Xiangcun
Liu, Lina
Zhang, Zhuanfang
Chai, Dongfeng
Zhao, Ming
Zhang, Wenzhi
Li, Jinlong
coredata.dc:description: Developing earth-abundant transition metal-based oxygen evolution reaction (OER) electrocatalysts is extremely essential for accomplishing efficient electrocatalytic overall water splitting. Herein, we synthesized a Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs catalyst with a microstructure composed of interlaced nanowires in a spherical-chain shape by treating a nickel foam substrate with sulfur and co-regulating the morphology and properties of manganese dioxide with carbon dots. The catalyst exhibited excellent OER electrocatalytic performance and stability under alkaline conditions, requiring only a low overpotential of 226 mV at 10 mA cm-2 and a Tafel slope of 105.76 mV dec-1. Furthermore, the catalyst remained stable after 3000 cycles of Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) testing and multistep chronopotentiometric (CP) measurements at the current density of 10, 50 and 100 mA cm−2 for 12 h, respectively. The enhanced performance of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs catalyst was mainly attributed to the practical improvement of charge interface transport performance induced by carbon dots and interface layer Ni3S2 which increased active catalytic sites of the electrode and reduced charge transfer resistance. These results suggest a promising approach for developing efficient OER electrocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalytic
Oxygen evolution reaction (OER)
Catalysts
Hydrogen production
Water splitting
Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs catalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,F-CQDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and structural characterizations of the Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs composite materials. (A, B) SEM, (C, D) TEM, (E) SAED pattern, and (F) EDS elemental mapping images.
Structural characterization of the Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs sample. (A) XRD patterns. (B–I) XPS spectra: survey, Mn 2p, Ni 2p, S 2p, O 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and F 1s.
(A) FTIR spectra, (B) Raman spectra, (C) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms, and (D) Pore size distribution curves.

Catalytic activity of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs in 1.0 M KOH solution for the OER. (A) LSV curves. (B) Overpotentials. (C) Histogram of the onset potential. (D) The corresponding Tafel plots are derived from the LSVs of (A). (E) Nyquist plots. (F) Cdl values. (G) TOF plots for Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs, and Ni3S2/NF in the OER process. (H) LSV curves of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs before and after 3000 cycles CV. (I) Multistep CP measurements of the long-term stability of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs at the current density of 10, 50 and 100 mA cm−2 for 12 h, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of the Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs composite materials.

head.ce:title: A novel Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs composite with spherical-chain-like morphology for efficient oxygen evolution reaction in aqueous alkaline solutions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Catalytic Synthesis for Fine Chemicals, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Catalytic Synthesis for Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Catalytic Synthesis for Fine Chemicals, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, P. R

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing earth-abundant transition metal-based oxygen evolution reaction (OER) electrocatalysts is extremely essential for accomplishing efficient electrocatalytic overall water splitting. Herein, we synthesized a Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs catalyst with a microstructure composed of interlaced nanowires in a spherical-chain shape by treating a nickel foam substrate with sulfur and co-regulating the morphology and properties of manganese dioxide with carbon dots. The catalyst exhibited excellent OER electrocatalytic performance and stability under alkaline conditions, requiring only a low overpotential of 226 mV at 10 mA cm-2 and a Tafel slope of 105.76 mV dec-1. Furthermore, the catalyst remained stable after 3000 cycles of Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) testing and multistep chronopotentiometric (CP) measurements at the current density of 10, 50 and 100 mA cm−2 for 12 h, respectively. The enhanced performance of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs catalyst was mainly attributed to the practical improvement of charge interface transport performance induced by carbon dots and interface layer Ni3S2 which increased active catalytic sites of the electrode and reduced charge transfer resistance. These results suggest a promising approach for developing efficient OER electrocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, increasingly exhausted fossil fuels and the sustainably deteriorated environment severely impede the sustainable development and survival of contemporary human beings. Hydrogen shows promising potential to replace fossil fuels as a clean and renewable energy source [1–3]. Currently, the widely utilized hydrogen production technologies primarily depend on non-renewable energy sources such as natural gas, petroleum, coal, etc, which emit carbon dioxide into the atmosphere during the production process [4]. Overall water splitting can be a competitive alternative for hydrogen production, which can accomplish by using renewable and environmentally friendly energy sources, such as wind or solar power. As all we know, the overall water splitting is mainly composed of the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). In sharp contrast with the HER, the OER is a multiple-step four-electron reaction process, which requires higher overpotential and more power due to the sluggish kinetics [5]. The introduction of suitable electrocatalysts with high efficiency can effectively decrease the overpotential and accelerate the kinetics by varying the reaction process and reducing the reaction activation energy. Up to now, the high-efficient electrocatalyst for improving the OER property mainly concentrates on noble metal oxides such as RuO2 and IrO2, which makes large-scale commercialization challenging for economic hydrogen production due to the high cost and scarcity. Therefore, it is imperative to explore and engineer the OER electrocatalysts with high efficiency, low-cost and abundant materials [6–8].

In recent decades, many efforts have been focused on the transition metal-based compounds (e.g., oxides, oxyhydroxides, and chalcogenide) due to their numerous inherent advantages, such as earth abundance, cost-efficiency and superior electrocatalytic performance for OER [9,10]. Among them, manganese-based electrocatalysts have captured tremendous attention owing to their desirable merits, for instance, non-toxicity and less expensive [11]. Particularly, MnO2, as one of the most commonly known manganese oxides, is considered as a potential candidate for OER electrocatalyst in view of its distinctive advantages, such as structural flexibility and high electrochemical stability, and catalytic activity [12]. However, its electrocatalytic active highly rely on the adopted synthetic route and conditions coupled with the chemical stoichiometry, which will give rise to a serious influence on the ratio of Mn3+/Mn4+ in the crystal structure, crystallographic phase, morphology, defect concentration, specific surface area, and pore structure. Besides, unsatisfactory conductivity and poor stability are also harsh limitations for gaining high OER activity [13–15]. Thus, its electrocatalytic OER active is still uncompetitive to the precious metal based electrocatalyst so far as to the other 3d transition metals-based electrocatalysts (e.g., nickel, cobalt, iron). Consequently, numerous efforts have been focused on the engineering and optimization of the OER performance of MnO2 for efficient water splitting. To the best of our knowledge, the introduction of high conductivity materials and the construction of heterojunction can efficiently regulate the morphology or synchronously ameliorate the electrocatalytic performance of the resulting catalysts. Furthermore, our previous works also demonstrated that the simultaneous integration of biochar and heterojunction could enhance the photocatalytic activity of ZnO and the incorporation of CQDs can improve the final electrocatalytic activity of MnSxSe1-x, in which the morphologies of final catalysts also show apparent evolution compared with the pure counterparts [16]. Accordingly, it's also very believable that the electrocatalytic performance of MnO2 can also be improved by adopting the strategies as mentioned above. Additionally, the performance of conductive materials and the selection of another catalyst for heterojunction construction is a critical consideration for obtaining the high efficiency of OER performance.

Herein, we firstly designed a novel strategy to improve the electrocatalytic performance of MnO2 toward OER. As exhibited in Scheme 1 , the Ni3S2 was initially prepared by in situ hydrothermal treatment of nickel foam (NF) matrix, and then the N,F-CQDS-modified MnO2 was deposited on the as-prepared Ni3S2/NF via a in situ crystallization induction process utilizing the reaction between hemp-derived N,F-CQDs, and KMnO4 solution, the final product was denoted as Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs. Benefiting from the N,F-CQDs induction and utilization of as-prepared Ni3S2/NF as formation substrate, the Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs show a distinctive spherical-chain-like morphology, improved specific surface area and ameliorated pore structure. Importantly, compared with the pure MnO2 (overpotential of 417 mV at 10 mA cm-2 and Tafel slope of 179.74 mV dec-1), the resulting Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs exhibit a significantly enhanced electrocatalytic properties in alkaline condition, delivering a lower overpotential of 226 mV at 10 mA cm-2 and a slight Tafel slope of 105.76 mV dec-1. The optimized OER performance was mainly attributed to the integration of N,F-CQDs, and Ni3S2 heterojunction, which can simultaneously improve the conductivity, reduce the charges transfer resistance, enlarge the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA), etc. Moreover, the obtained Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs maintained strong long-term electrocatalytic stability, which can continuously operating for 12 h at current densities of 10, 50, and 100 mA cm-2, there was no significant trend in voltage change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,F-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,F-CQDs) were prepared by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Specifically, industrial hemp powder was pulverized twice in a cell disruptor, and 0.80 g of the fine Crushed powder and 0.30 g of NH4F were dispersed sequentially in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol. The solution was transferred into a high-pressure vessel lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and kept at 180 °C for 30 min in a sealed microwave synthesis reactor. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the resulting black suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm nylon membrane filter and further dialyzed in ethanol for 6 h. The solution was then evaporated using a rotary evaporator, and the obtained product was dried at 90 °C for 24 h. Finally, N,F-CQDs powder sample was obtained by grinding in an agate mortar.

In this work, the clean nickel foam (abbreviated as NF) was employed as the conductive matrix for the immobilization of the electrocatalysts. Before the growth of the catalyst, the NF with a size of 1 × 2 cm2 was rinsed by a consecutive ultrasonic treatment procedure with acetone, 6 M HCl, deionized water, and ethanol for 15 min respectively and then dried in a vacuum oven, at 50 °C for 12 h. The pre-treated nickel foam was immersed in 10 mL of 1.445 mM CH4N2S solution, then transferred together into a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The reaction vessel was sealed and maintained at 150 °C for 5 h. After the high-pressure reaction vessel was cooled to room temperature, the nickel foam was washed several times with deionized water and dried at 50 °C for 10 h, finally, the precursor Ni3S2/NF was obtained.

To prepare Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs composite material, 0.04 g of N,F-CQDs powder was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water under ultrasonication and the solution termed as A, then 0.30 g of KMnO4 was dissolved in 30 mL distilled water under ultrasonication and the solution termed as B. Then, solution A was gradually dropped into solution B under ultrasonication, after that the mixture was stirred for 15 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. Finally, the mixed solution and the prepared Ni3S2/NF were transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 150 °C for 3 h in a sealed microwave synthesis reactor. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting black nickel foam was washed several times with deionized water and dried at 50 °C for 24 h to obtain Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs composite material. Comparison samples labeled as Ni3S2/NF, MnO2@N,F-CQDs, and MnO2, were prepared under the same conditions.
Additional experimental details, including materials, characterization, and electrochemical measurements, can be found in the Supporting Information.

The micromorphology and structure of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs were investigated employing scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The SEM images of the Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs catalyst revealed a nanosphere structure consisting of aggregated one-dimensional chain-like nanowires with a diameter size of approximately 400 nm (as shown in Fig. 1 A and B). Similarly, the TEM image confirmed that the nanosphere structure is composed of a large number of nanowires, which is consistent with the SEM observations (Fig. 1C). By contrast, as shown inFig. S2, A and B, the morphologies of Ni3S2 nanosheet-assembled-spikes and MnO2@N,F-CQDs nanosheet-assembled-nanoflowers, respectively, demonstrating the transformation of sample morphology through experimental design. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were used to investigate the internal crystal structure of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs. Lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.20 nm, 0.24 nm, and 0.23 nm were observed, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite, the (003) plane of Ni3S2, and the (222) plane of MnO2, respectively, confirming the successful synthesis of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs (Fig. 1D). Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) spectra in-situ analysis were performed to further reveal the crystalline phase of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs. Two bright rings observed in the SAED pattern indicate the polycrystalline nature of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs (the illustration of Fig. 1D), which are consistent with TEM results. The EDS element mapping results further confirmed the successful preparation of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs, with Mn, Ni, S, O, C, N, and F elements distributed uniformly (Fig. 1E).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were utilized to investigate the crystal structure of the Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs, Ni3S2/NF, and MnO2@N,F-CQDs catalysts. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the diffraction peaks located at 21.75°, 38.27°, 50.12, 55.34°corresponded to the (101), (021), (211), (300) crystal planes of Ni3S2 (JCPDF #44-1418), respectively. The diffraction peaks located at 19.11°, 37.12°, 45.07°, and 65.70° corresponded to the (111), (311), (400), and (440) crystal planes of MnO2 (JCPDF#44-0992), respectively. The absence of diffraction peaks for N,F-CQDs in the XRD pattern could be ascribed to the lower content, resulting in reduced diffraction peak intensity. This is consistent with the results of SAED, which confirms the successful synthesis of the catalyst.

In addition, the chemical state and element composition of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 3B–I shows the XPS survey spectrum and the high-resolution XPS spectra (Mn 2p, Ni 2p, S 2p, O 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and F 1s core energy levels) of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs composite. As revealed in Fig. 3 B, the main elements present in Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs are Mn, Ni, S, O, C, N, and F. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Mn 2p in Fig. 3C exhibit two main peaks near 641.8 eV and 653.5 eV, corresponding to the Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 orbitals, respectively [17,18]. The binding energies at 641.5 eV and 652.9 eV correspond to the Mn3+, while the peaks centered at 642.9 eV and 654.3 eV can be attributed to Mn4+. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ni 2p, the two peaks at binding energies of 873.7 eV and 856.1 eV correspond to Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2 of Ni2+, respectively, and the corresponding spin-induced satellite peaks are located at 879.7 eV and 861.7 eV (Fig. 3D) [19]. In Fig. 3E, the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 peaks are located at binding energies of 162.1 eV and 163.0 eV, respectively, and the peak at 168.8 eV is attributed to SO4 2− [20]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s in Fig. 2F show three convolution peaks located at 529.6 eV, 530 eV, and 531.8 eV, correspond to Mn–O, Mn–O–C, and CO bonds, respectively [21]. Fig. 3G shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, the peaks centered at 284.85 eV, 288.70 eV, 292.39 eV, and 295.23 eV, can be ascribed to C–C, C–N, C–O, and O–CO bonds, respectively [22]. In the N 1s XPS spectrum, the peaks centered at binding energies of 399.5 eV, 400.5 eV, and 401.4 eV correspond to C–N–C, N-(C)3, and N–H bonds, respectively (Fig. 2H) [23]. Additionally, the high-resolution XPS spectra of F 1s are shown in Fig. 3I, the peaks located at 684.8 eV and 688.5 eV, correspond to F–Mn and C–F bonds, respectively, confirming the successful synthesis of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs [24].

To further understand the properties of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs, FT-IR, Raman, and N2 adsorption-desorption tests were performed to characterize the catalysts (Fig. 4 ). The FT-IR spectra of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs, and MnO2@N,F-CQDs catalysts are shown in Fig. 4A. The peaks at 718 cm-1, 591 cm-1, and 990-1299 cm-1 in the FT-IR spectrum correspond to the stretching vibrations of Mn–O and C–N, while the peaks centered at 1616 cm-1 and 3446 cm-1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of Mn–O–Mn and O–H, respectively [25]. For Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs, the characteristic peak at 473 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the Ni–O bond, and the characteristic peak of MnO2 is also clearly observed, indicating the coexistence of both materials [26]. As present in Fig. 4B, the Raman spectrum of MnO2@N,F-CQDs shows a peak at 633 cm-1, which is attributed to the Mn–O symmetric stretching vibration of the MnO6 plane in MnO2 [27]. The Raman spectrum of Ni3S2 shows three characteristic peaks centered at 202 cm-1, 304 cm-1, and 350 cm-1, respectively [28]. In contrast, the Raman peaks of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs are shifted to lower wavenumbers, composed of peaks centered at 196 cm-1, 295 cm-1, 340 cm-1, and 630 cm-1. As exhibited in Fig. 4C, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curve displays the typical IV-type isotherm with a hysteresis loop of H3, indicating the presence of mesopores in the Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs composite material according to the IUPAC classification [29]. The BET surface area of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs is more significant than that of MnO2@N,F-CQDs, reaching up to 202.7 m2 g-1. As shown in Fig. 4D, the pore size of MnO2@N,F-CQDs is only 2.5–30 nm, while the pore size of the Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs composite material is concentrated in the range of 4–30 nm. These results indicate that the abundant surface area and mesopores can provide abundant active sites for the oxygen evolution reaction, thereby improving the OER performance.

The electrocatalytic OER performance of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs, were evaluated in 1 M KOH solution using a three-electrode system, which the catalyst, graphite electrode, and Hg/HgO electrode were used as working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode, respectively. Besides, the OER activities of Ni3S2/NF, MnO2@N,F-CQDs and MnO2 were also assessed under the same conditions. Fig. 4A shows the LSV curves of these catalyst, Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs exhibits the best OER performance with overpotentials of 226 and 295 mV at current densities of 10 and 50 mA cm-2, respectively, which are much lower than those of Ni3S2/NF (η10 = 275 mV), MnO2@N,F-CQDs (η10 = 344 mV), and MnO2 (η10 = 417 mV) (Fig. 4A and B). As shown in Fig. 4C, the onset potential of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs is the lowest, only 1.42 V, compared to Ni3S2/NF (1.46 V), MnO2@N,F-CQDs (1.54 V), and MnO2 (1.70 V). To further investigate the chemical reaction kinetics of the catalysts, Tafel slopes were analyzed. As indicated in Fig. 4D, Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs has the smallest Tafel slope (105.76 mV·dec-1), which is smaller than those of Ni3S2/NF (116.28 mV·dec-1), MnO2@N,F-CQDs (131.15 mV·dec-1), and MnO2 (179.74 mV·dec-1). This result indicates that Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs has the best reaction kinetics. In addition, compared with the OER performance of MnO2-based composite electrocatalysts reported in the current literature (Table S1), Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs also exhibited lower overpotential and excellent electrocatalytic activity.

In order to further investigate the influence of charge transfer characteristics on the electrocatalytic activity of the catalyst, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted, and the results are shown in Fig. 4E. The charge transfer resistance (R ct) of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs is significantly lower than Ni3S2/NF, MnO2@N,F-CQDs, and MnO2 composite materials, indicating that Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs has smaller interface resistance and faster charge transfer rate. To explore the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the catalyst, the Cdl value obtained from the CV curve was analyzed. As described in Fig. S3, the CV curves of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs, Ni3S2/NF, MnO2@N,F-CQDs, and MnO2 were measured at different scan rates (20–100 mV s-1) in the non-Faraday voltage range. As can be seen from the data in Fig. 4F, compared with the Cdl values of Ni3S2/NF (16.38 mF cm-2), MnO2@N,F-CQD (10.18 mF cm-2), and MnO2 (6.99 mF cm-2), the Cdl value of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs was the highest at 27.88 mF cm-2. The ECSA values of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs, Ni3S2/NF, MnO2@N,F-CQDs, and MnO2 were calculated from the obtained Cdl values, which were 697 cm-2, 409 cm-2, 254 cm-2, and 175 cm-2, respectively, confirming that Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs has a larger active surface area and more abundant catalytic active sites. TOF curves were used to analyze the intrinsic electrocatalytic activity of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. S4B, based on the measured LSV curve and CV curve in 1.0 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH ≈ 7), the TOF curve of the catalyst was obtained as exhibited in Fig. 4G. As manifested in Fig. S4B, the TOF value of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs at a potential of 1.53 V is 0.3053 s-1, which is 4.3 times higher than that of Ni3S2/NF (0.0713 s-1), indicating the better intrinsic electrocatalytic activity of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs toward OER. To further investigate the feasibility of practical applications of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs, long-term OER stability tests were conducted. As demonstrated in Fig. 4H, there is only a slight deviation in the LSV curve before and after 3000 CV cycles. Meanwhile, as exhibited in Fig. 4I, in the multistep CP testing, after continuous operation for 12 h under current densities of 10, 50, and 100 mA cm-2, respectively, there was no significant trend in potential change, indicates that Ni3S2/MnO2@N, F-CQDs exhibit strong electrochemical stability.

In summary, we prepared a nano-ball-shaped Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs electrocatalyst composed of chain-like nanowires by a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method under the induction of N,F-CQDs. The unique composition and structure of Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs increase the contact area of the reactants in the electrolyte, providing a strong driving force to accelerate the charge transfer rate of the electrocatalyst. In alkaline media, Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs exhibited excellent OER electrocatalytic activity and stability, with an overpotential of 226 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm-2 and a Tafel slope of 105.76 mV·dec-1. Furthermore, it remained stable after 3000 cycles of CV and multistep CP measurements at the current density of 10, 50 and 100 mA cm−2 working continuously for 12 h, respectively. The improved performance of the Ni3S2/MnO2@N,F-CQDs electrocatalyst is mainly attributed to the practical improvement of charge transfer performance by introducing N,F-CQDs with high electronegativity and the interface layer of Ni3S2. This work provides a new approach to the development of the high-performance transition metal-based OER catalysts.

Fan Yang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Visualization, Methodology. Beibei Sun: Investigation, Conceptualization, Data curation. Guohua Dong: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Project administration. Xiangcun Liu: Resources, Data curation. Lina Liu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Zhuanfang Zhang: Formal analysis. Dongfeng Chai: Validation. Ming Zhao: Funding acquisition. Wenzhi Zhang: Resources, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Jinlong Li: Resources, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: No conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and the manuscript is approved by all authors for publication. I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere in whole or in part. All the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32272823), Special Funds from the Central Finance to Support the Development of Local Universities, the Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Heilongjiang Province of China (Grant No. 135509505), Special Funds from the Central Finance to Support the Development of Local Universities, the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province of China (Grant No. LH2019B020), Degree and Postgraduate Education Teaching Reform Research Project of Qiqihar University (Grant No. JGXM_QUG_2021017) and Educational Science Research Project of Qiqihar University (Grant No. GJQTyB202139).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-alkaline water-splitting activity of mesoporous 3D heterostructures: an amorphous-shell@crystalline-core nano-assembly of Co-Ni-phosphate ultrathin-nanosheets and V- doped cobalt-nitride nanowires
Adv. Sci.
